concept.headline: Borrow a value with & to use it without taking ownership.
concept.explanation: Instead of moving a value into a function, you can lend it via a reference. An immutable reference (&T) lets the borrower read the value; a mutable reference (&mut T) lets it modify the value. Rust enforces two rules: you can have any number of &T references at once, OR exactly one &mut T — but never both simultaneously. This statically prevents data races.
keyPoints: &T is a shared (immutable) reference — multiple allowed simultaneously
&mut T is an exclusive (mutable) reference — only one allowed at a time
You cannot have &mut T and any &T in the same scope at the same time
References must always be valid — Rust prevents dangling references
Borrowing does not transfer ownership — the original owner retains it

demo.intro: Watch a function borrow a String immutably (read), then another function borrow it mutably (write).

explanation.why: We declare s as mut because we plan to mutably borrow it later (append needs &mut s)
explanation.intent: Set up the String we will both read from and modify
explanation.misconception: let mut s means the binding s can be reassigned AND we can take &mut references to it. Without mut on the binding, you cannot create a &mut reference.

explanation.what: Calling length with an immutable borrow &s
explanation.why: length only needs to read s.len() — an immutable reference is sufficient and does not affect ownership
explanation.change: temporary &String borrow created; s still owned by main
explanation.intent: Demonstrate reading via reference without moving the value
explanation.misconception: Passing &s creates a borrow, not a copy. The function sees the same heap data as main but cannot modify it.

explanation.why: s.len() inside length() reads the length field of the String metadata — 'hello' has 5 bytes
explanation.intent: Capture the result of reading via a reference
explanation.misconception: After length() returns, the borrow &s is released — s is fully accessible again in main

explanation.why: len holds the usize 5 returned by the length function
explanation.intent: Show the string length was read successfully via a borrow
explanation.misconception: println! borrows len for reading — usize is Copy so it could also be printed by value

explanation.what: Calling append with a mutable borrow &mut s
explanation.why: append needs to push bytes onto the heap buffer — it requires exclusive write access via &mut
explanation.change: exclusive &mut String borrow created; no other borrows of s may exist
explanation.intent: Modify the String through a mutable reference without transferring ownership
explanation.misconception: You cannot hold any &s borrow at the same time as &mut s — Rust would reject that at compile time

explanation.what: push_str appends ' world' to s's heap buffer
explanation.why: The mutable borrow allows the function to write to the heap allocation
explanation.intent: Show that mutation via &mut modifies the original, not a copy
explanation.misconception: push_str does not create a new String — it extends the existing heap buffer in place (if capacity allows)

explanation.why: The &mut borrow was released when append() returned — s is fully owned by main again, now with value 'hello world'
explanation.intent: Confirm that the mutation via &mut s affected the original
explanation.misconception: s was never moved — only borrowed. main retained ownership throughout and gets the modified value back.

options: Compile error: cannot have &mut s while &s is active
Compile error: s is not mutable
prediction.explanation: r1 holds an immutable borrow of s. While r1 is still in use (on the println! line), taking &mut s is illegal — Rust's borrow checker rejects simultaneous shared and mutable borrows.

explanation.afterDemo: length borrowed &s to read the length without affecting ownership. Once length returned, the borrow expired and main could take a &mut borrow for append. The two borrows never overlapped — that is why the code compiled. If you had tried to use r1 and &mut s at the same time, the compiler would have caught it.
keyTakeaways: &T allows many simultaneous readers; &mut T requires exclusive access.
Borrowing does not transfer ownership — the owner retains it and gets it back when the borrow ends.
Rust checks borrow conflicts at compile time — no runtime cost.

practice.prompt: Write a function `double_it` that takes &mut i32 and multiplies the value by 2. In main, declare a mutable variable `n = 5`, call double_it, and print n.
practice.hint: To write through a mutable reference, dereference with *: *n *= 2;

feedback.successMessage: Correct! *n *= 2 dereferences the mutable reference to write through it. n in main becomes 10.
retryHints: Use *n to dereference the mutable reference — n inside the function is a &mut i32, not an i32.

summary.title: References & Borrowing — Complete
takeaways: &T gives read-only access to a value without taking ownership.
&mut T gives exclusive write access — no other borrows may coexist.
The borrow checker enforces these rules at compile time with zero runtime cost.
